other_observers: Brenda Linfield and I found and first observed the bird; Bryan Grundy took photos shortly afterwards and his photos are included with Brenda's submission
description: When I first saw the bird in binoculars, it was perched on a snag in the middle of the lake. The size, shape, and mostly white color immediately told me it was a Sterna tern.   Observed through a spotting scope, the long, thin, black bill with pale yellow tip stood out dramatically. The bird had a clean, white body with a solid black cap and black legs. The wings were nearly pure white when the bird was perched.  Observed in flight, the upper surface of the tail was pure white, and the tail was forked. The upper surface of the wings was white with a black patch near the ends. The underside of the wings was mostly or entirely white.Behavior: Most of the time, it was perched on a snag. When it flew, it stayed within a small area, banking and spreading its wings/tail.Call: no vocalizations heardPlumage: breeding
